steps.title: Identify the relationship between the two halves of the text
steps.content:
The first part describes fish mimicking schoolmates fleeing a predator — a cascade that "benefits the collective." The second part describes sandpipers abandoning safe nesting grounds because of false alarms — a cascade with "unfavorable results." These two outcomes directly contradict each other: one cascade helps, the other harms. The transition must signal that opposition.
  • Choice A — 'In other words,': Signals a restatement or clarification of the same idea, but the second part presents a contrasting outcome, not a paraphrase. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Conversely,': Signals a direct contrast — exactly what is needed when the first example shows benefit and the second shows harm. ✓
  • Choice C — 'Indeed,': Signals agreement or emphasis of the same idea; it would imply the sandpiper example confirms the fish example, which it does not. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Consequently,': Signals a cause-and-effect relationship; the sandpiper situation is not a consequence of the fish study. ✗

steps.title: Verify the logical fit of the chosen transition
steps.content: Inserting "Conversely" reads: "…an information cascade that benefits the collective. Conversely, the animal world is at times susceptible to the spread of misinformation, often with unfavorable results…" The word correctly signals the flip from benefit to harm. No other option handles that direct opposition. That matches choice B — B is correct.
